Now, one thing I want to make crystal clear to you though is that what looping is not is about turning nose into yeses. That's a fool's errand. You see, what I do not want you to do is to get into these long protracted closing boughs with prospects who keep saying no, no, no, no, no. In fact, if you're using the system correctly, you should hardly ever be hearing the word no at the back half of the sale. People will give you that hard no, aren't even the least bit interested in what you have to offer, and they should have been qualified out long before this during the prospecting process, right? What you should be hearing at this point isn't the word no, but all the typical objections in the stalls and the smoke screens that you get from someone who's sitting on the fence because they're not 100% certain. What looping will allow you to do is take those objections and deal with them effectively. You get through a process called deflection, if the objection is a complete stall or a smoke stream, or by answering it head on if it happens to be a real objection, and then seamlessly transitioning to a language pattern that will raise their level of certainty for one or perhaps all three tens if necessary, and also adding on that little bit of pain that might be needed to lower the action threshold. And then of course you ask for the order again and again and again repeating that process loop after loop after loop. And each time you repeat this process, it's called doing a loop, which means you're essentially using your prospects objections as launching points for building more and more certainty for the three tens, and then lowering their action threshold as opposed to falling into a trap of getting into an argument with your client or a test of rules where each of you is trying to convince the other that you're right and they're wrong, which you completely shies or poor and cause you to blow the sale completely. Make sense?
